nativeRange.habitat: Found in sluggish tributaries, small rivers, and swampy blackwater zones, typically in areas with dense aquatic vegetation, submerged woody debris, and leaf litter. The water is usually tannin-stained, with a dark tea-like colour and very low mineral content.

decorPreferences: heavily planted with fine-leaved plants (Java moss, floating plants)
dark substrate to reduce stress and enhance coloration
dried Indian almond (catappa) leaves for tannins and microbe colonies

diet.notes: A micro-predator in nature, feeding on tiny invertebrates and zooplankton. In the aquarium, offer high-quality small dried foods such as micro pellets and crushed flakes, alongside daily portions of live or frozen micro-foods including Artemia nauplii, Daphnia, Moina, micro bloodworms, white mosquito larvae, and grindal worms. The mouth is small, so all foods must be appropriately tiny — large frozen or dry foods are not suitable.

goodWith: small peaceful characins (ember tetras, neon tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) as dither fish
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that may outcompete at feeding
cherry shrimp and dwarf shrimp (predation risk from adults)
fast-moving fish that cause stress in timid shoals

imageCandidates.notes: Guérin Nicolas; 1024x768. Two individuals in oblique snout-up posture with the bold black mid-lateral stripe running onto the caudal fin — group shot showing the species' diagnostic features clearly.
Lochrian; 640x480. Single live fish in a planted tank showing the reddish-bordered black lateral stripe and oblique posture; slightly soft aquarium-glass shot.

summary: The One-Lined Pencilfish (Nannostomus unifasciatus) is a slender Amazonian characin distinguished by a bold black mid-lateral stripe and an unusual oblique swim posture, with the snout angled upward toward the surface. It requires soft, acidic blackwater conditions and is best kept in groups of eight or more.
careNotes:
The One-Lined Pencilfish is one of only two Nannostomus species that adopts a pronounced oblique, snout-up swimming posture — angling the body at roughly 45° with the head tilted toward the surface — which sets it apart visually from the horizontal resting posture of most pencilfish. A single bold black lateral stripe runs the full body length onto the caudal fin, bordered above by a narrow gold or copper iridescent band. Like all pencilfish, it shifts to a cryptic, vertically-barred pattern at night, a color-change that follows the day-night cycle reliably and occurs even in blind specimens.

This species demands soft, acidic water in the blackwater tradition of the Amazon: a target pH of 5.0–6.5, hardness below 8 gH, and temperature of 24–27°C are recommended for long-term health. Hard or alkaline tap water tends to suppress coloration and immune function; reverse osmosis water remineralized to very low hardness, supplemented with Indian almond leaves or driftwood for tannins, is strongly recommended. Gentle flow is essential — an air-powered sponge filter is well suited to this species.

Groups of at least eight are advisable; smaller groups tend to produce timid, washed-out fish that hide rather than display naturally. A species tank or a carefully matched community with similarly small, soft-water species — such as ember tetras, Corydoras catfish, or Apistogramma dwarf cichlids — works well. The species occupies the upper-to-middle water column, where it also serves as a useful dither fish (a calm, visible species whose relaxed behavior signals to shyer tankmates that no predator is nearby) for dwarf cichlids.

The mouth is small, so all foods must be appropriately tiny. Baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Daphnia, Moina, and micro bloodworms are staple live or frozen options. High-quality micro pellets and crushed flakes are generally accepted once the fish have settled in. Strong surface agitation should be avoided, as it can disperse food before the fish can reach it.
breedingNotes: The One-Lined Pencilfish stands out among pencilfish as the only Nannostomus species kept in the aquarium hobby for which no confirmed captive spawning has been documented, despite the species being maintained in aquaria since at least the 1920s. The reasons remain unclear; leading hypotheses point to water chemistry requirements — extreme softness and acidity — that may not have been closely enough replicated, or to undiscovered environmental triggers. Close relatives such as N. eques and N. beckfordi scatter small adhesive eggs on the surfaces of fine-leaved plants or floating plants in very soft, acidic water under dim lighting, with males occasionally guarding briefly afterward. Any serious attempt to breed N. unifasciatus should replicate extreme blackwater conditions — a pH of 4.5–5.5, hardness below 2 gH, temperature of 26–28°C, tannin-stained water — with dense fine vegetation and minimal disturbance.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Nannostomus unifasciatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (pH 4.0–7.0, hardness 18–179 ppm, temperature 23–28°C), adult size (35–40 mm SL), tank dimensions (75 × 30 cm minimum), native range, habitat, diet, behavior (oblique swim posture, night coloration), compatibility, and the notable finding that no captive spawning has been recorded.

additional.name: FishBase — Nannostomus unifasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Lebiasinidae, Characiformes), maximum size (3.8 cm SL), temperature range (25–28°C), native distribution (Amazon basin of Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia; upper Orinoco basin of Venezuela; Guyana), and diet (worms, crustaceans, insects).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pencilfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General pencilfish husbandry guide covering temperature 24–27°C, pH 6.5–7.5, minimum tank 10–29 gal, diet (baby brine shrimp, cyclops, daphnia, crushed flakes), compatibility with corydoras/tetras/rasboras, and avoidance of shrimp. N. unifasciatus listed among covered species.

additional.name: Aquadiction — One-Lined Pencilfish Profile
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Confirmed oblique swim posture, single black lateral stripe with gold border, group size recommendation of 10+, soft acidic water preference, and compatible tankmates (tetras, corydoras, loricarids, killifish, Apistogramma, discus).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Nannostomus unifasciatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked etymology (uni = one, fasciatus = banded), species description history (Steindachner 1876), distribution including introduced Trinidad and Tobago population, and oblique posture shared with N. eques as one of only two Nannostomus species with this characteristic.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive spawning has been recorded, despite the species being kept in aquaria since at least the 1920s. Any serious breeding attempt should replicate extreme soft-acid blackwater conditions, targeting a pH of around 4.5 to 5.5 and a hardness below 2 gH. Related species scatter small adhesive eggs on the surfaces of fine-leaved plants.
